item.title: Signature Dish
item.content:encoded:
A foodchain with pineapple guacamole as a signature dish is intrinsecally evil.
We can forgive worlddestroying crimes, but foodcrimes NO! ("yes, I'm italian, how you guessed?")
Seriously, very interesting, a different perspective on Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this was a great read… until the audio log. And although I, too, feel a bit sad about "those guys that just try to do business", I find that particular addendum insufferable. Now I don't have much experience in writing, and no experience in writing SCPs so I can't even tell how it could be changed for better (not that it should, considering the relatively high number of upvotes) but I can't help but think of it as that part of a bad novel when the author just dumps all the exposition in one, big, indigestible chunk so that all the dumb readers follow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're trying so hard, poor guys

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey I thought I was a paladin, not a doctor! I really enjoyed this one, I love the idea of a keter SCP that isn’t malevolent but only incompetent. That recorded conversation between the 1141-1s makes me wonder why the Foundation hasn’t tried to communicate to them some details on the laws of our universe or at least giving them a clearer idea of what entails a habitable environment for us semi-bald apes. They did sound like they aren’t likely to listen, but I think it would be worth a shot.

item.title: update
item.content:encoded: I've updated this article with a new CC-compliant picture, courtesy of myself, and I've tweaked the dialogue a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: double headcanon: these guys are from the same universe as bob.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The audio log ruined it for me. Completely and utterly killed any and all mystery, as well as coming across as amazingly fake dialogue expo-dumping all over the rest of the article. have to downvote for that =(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I live in New England. Need I say more? XD

And honestly, this is kind of sad. Not sad as in a pathetic article, I mean the whole situation makes me feel sad for Duane. If he really is the same species as "that incorporeal thing that hates humans" (I can't recall off the top of my head which scip that is), then his species isn't all bad, they just have different opinions - like humans! And this guy just wants to feed humans food. He's a human-designed-animal-contaiment-facility and human-food nerd, and his dream is to be part of both aspects of human culture. It's cute, and it's so sad that they keep messing up the fundamental physics of the thing. +1 hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'll find it's the very next one in line, SCP-1142.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but the article seems to be using the word "dimension" as another term for "universe". This came up on another discussion page, though the article itself escapes me…it was the one where something was broadcasting a distress signal from an alternate-history WW2 where the Axis apparently summoned something that was causing their world big problems. The article itself originally used the word "dimension" to refer to the alternate universe until someone pointed out that's not what a dimension is at all, despite popular usage indicating such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved the explanation at the end. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This is great. I didn't understand it until the note, but I upvoted it before then, anyway. Loved everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I actually get away with it because A) it's a good article, and B) there's a general trend towards experimenting more with the format, and C) most people don't actually care too much about technical stuff if the piece is still enjoyable (see: SCP-1230).

Alterius is latin for "other", which just serves to signify that it's from "somewhere else" — be that another universe, or simply a place that we have no information on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Breaks standards and harks back to the time of hybrid object classes but you got away with it with your rep so…

What makes the object class different from regular Keter then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sad I missed this one previously! It's a lot of fun. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, I had the same thought. Probably came from the whole "wearing their skin" line.

Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reason is "because I can get away with it and want to encourage people to experiment with Object Classes as they see fit, without discouraging people from continuing to use the standard object classes". And it is something new.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any reason for the Latin object class? Is this something new?

item.title: Photo Attribution
item.content:encoded:
I took the accompanying photograph myself — at the Minnesota Zoo, if you're at all curious.

The old image was not CC-compatible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your stuff inspired that. I usually take inspiration from other articles when I want to figure out how to format something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did my stuff inspire that formatting or did I subconsciously get that from one on your things or something

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Briefly considered changing the [unintelligible]s into <UNKNOWN: "SRQNABOTF">s, but figured it would be a kind of wanky reference that wouldn't add much to the piece. Unless there's like, demand for it, in which case I would defer to the judgment of others.
Woo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woah… This freakin surprised me.
…
…Someday, someone needs to convince a new england zoo concession area staff member to read the SCP wiki and direct them to this article. Then on april fools day we can convince them to add Guacamole and Pineapple chunks to their menu, just for the day. Anyone who has read this article will begin looking at them veeeeeeery strangely.

…But anyways, take my upvote, please!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crappy zoo restaurant that can cause a vacuum metastability disaster. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, the spirit of extra dimensional capitalism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nobody goes to the zoo 'til the snow's mostly gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two or more aliens came to the earth with ambition but wrong physical rules food…Amazing idea and that two guys are so cute
Feels like it could have a tale somedays, Entrepreneurship Diary or something like that,hm.
But why 20% snow-covered?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd kind of guessed what was going on before I got to the audio log, but instead of the audio being repetitive I feel like it really helped establish the tone of the item, and was also pretty damn funny. Duane could do so many things to our universe, but all he wants is to start a generic zoo cafe and serve guacamole with pineapple chunks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought that the witticism of the extradimensionals was pretty great. It's also very well written and the addenda are nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I need to stop reading the forums in class. I had to stifle some pretty heavy laughing at this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had one job, Miranda! One. Job.

Betty nailed the mass psychology and camouflage, Duane's "tropical guac" is friggin' delicious! But you can't get a few simple cosmological constant right? I'm starting to question your commitment to this entire trans-dimensional-animal-display-area-based-snack-shack project, Miranda!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. These folks (or whatever) have unfathomable power over the laws of physics and the space-time continuum, which they apply… to food service. In zoos. In New England.

Reminds me a lot of dial-a-llama, in that sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You might be being pedantic, but I changed it anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You maybe need to rewrite that particular part then because I was just thinking of having 1x1 feet of snow in each zoo. I'm probably being pedantic though.
I personally think that the weeaboo angle makes the article though, because otherwise there seems no reason for them to be in our universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The weeaboo bit is sort of a bonus if you like the SCP on its own.

And keeping snow on the ground on every zoo in New England would take quite a bit of explaining to do. If it's anything like Minnesota, there are at least a few months where the ground is devoid of snow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think the fact that they're "interdimensional equivalent of weeaboos" becomes apparent throughout the article, even in the final log. Also, it doesn't manifest if there is snow on the ground? I can imagine that The Foundation would probably have the wherewithal to keep snow on the ground.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the audio log is the weakest part of this. It didn't sit well with me until the revelation at the end of how it's affected containment procedures. And then of course I came here and saw things like

interdimensional equivalent of a weeaboo

and

the same kind of entity as the incorporeal being that's racist against humans

and now it's amazing. I swear, if I ever see a concessions stand — anywhere — that offers guacamole with pineapple chunks, I will shit and run away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Antimatter ketchup? Ouch. That must have been some seriously hot sauce…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article… Just… Oh my gosh, I upvoted so hard. I especially like the audio log, that really pulls things together. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
?

It's definitely not the first two things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcanon: These are the same kind of entity as the incorporeal being that's racist against humans.

This pushed me towards upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. Clean, simple, and effective. I like it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Miranda's the one who keeps fucking up :P

GET YOUR SHIT TOGETHER, MIRANDA

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An extradimensional food stand. Love it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very amusing, I hope Duane gets the physics down right so he doesn't have to wake up with the fact that his restaurant has been classified Keter. Now I'm not saying to change the article or something like that, but I wish him and his family the best of luck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wasn't a huge fan (too similar to a lot of things, A Chain Restaurant comes to mind), but the last audio log was interesting enough to save it from a downvote.
No vote.
E: Hell, it's unique enough. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: love it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw this is great man +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy crap, you're not dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Smapti gets a cookie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. Now I won't take off of my ehad the image of beaurmont trying to get a job here and serving food to humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy it. Poor Duane. He's just trying to feed his inter-dimensional tentacle beast family…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon: These are the same kind of entity as the incorporeal being that's racist against humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Well, whaddaya know. There is a stand there. Musta missed it before. Anyway, like I was sayin', we were all over at Bill and Tammy's place watchin' the game…"

People have an amazing capacity to ignore that which should surprise them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't address it, actually, but that might be worth putting something in. I'll check it out.

E: Added. They don't know what's going on either. Of course, if everything's in place and it's on the maps, they might be tempted to think that they just missed it before. Who knows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ridiculous but fascinating! I didn't see it, but do you mention anywhere in the article about the reaction of workers/other vendor staff/people who are familiar with the zoo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I've been thinking for a while, you know how extradimensional people are shown as being physically compatible with our universe, but they think and act differently? Well, what if we had some stuff from another dimension that was completely normal and able to act just like us, but the laws of physics didn't work right for it?

That got tied up in this when I went to the St. Louis zoo over spring break and I saw that their crappy restaurants were essentially the same as in Minnesota. I think their predictability and blandness sort of add to the piece's coherence, which is why I used that to frame it.

Anyways, this is the story about the interdimensional equivalent of a weeaboo. Enjoy.

E: Someone remind me to give this a list name after the AFD edits get reverted.
